context.text: The first human landing on the Moon occurred on July 20, 1969.

context.text: Apollo 11 was the spaceflight that landed Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin on the lunar surface.

question: Which planet in our solar system is famous for its prominent ring system?

context.title: Planetary rings
context.text: Several planets have rings, but the most extensive system belongs to the sixth planet from the Sun.

context.title: Saturn
context.text: Saturn is a gas giant known for its bright and complex rings made of ice and dust.

question: What is the name of the first artificial satellite launched into Earth orbit?

context.title: Space Race
context.text: The Space Race began with the successful launch of a satellite by the Soviet Union in 1957.

context.title: Sputnik 1
context.text: Sputnik 1 was the first artificial Earth satellite, launched into an elliptical low Earth orbit.

question: Which force keeps the planets in orbit around the Sun?

context.title: Orbit
context.text: An orbit is the gravitationally curved trajectory of an object around a point in space.

context.title: Gravity
context.text: Gravity is the fundamental interaction which causes mutual attraction between all things with mass, including planets and the Sun.

question: What is the largest planet in our solar system?

context.title: Solar System
context.text: The Solar System consists of the Sun and the objects that orbit it, including eight planets.

context.title: Jupiter
context.text: Jupiter is the fifth planet from the Sun and the largest in the Solar System.

question: What is the name of the galaxy that contains our Solar System?

context.title: Galaxy
context.text: A galaxy is a gravitationally bound system of stars, stellar remnants, and interstellar gas.

context.title: Milky Way
context.text: The Milky Way is the galaxy that includes the Solar System, with the name describing the galaxy's appearance from Earth.

question: Which space agency developed the James Webb Space Telescope?

context.title: James Webb Space Telescope
context.text: The JWST is a space telescope designed to conduct infrared astronomy, succeeding the Hubble Space Telescope.

context.title: NASA
context.text: NASA led the development of the JWST in collaboration with the ESA and the CSA.

question: What is the name of the red supergiant star that is part of the constellation Orion?

context.title: Orion (constellation)
context.text: Orion is a prominent constellation located on the celestial equator and visible throughout the world.

context.title: Betelgeuse
context.text: Betelgeuse is usually the tenth-brightest star in the night sky and the second-brightest in the constellation of Orion.

question: What is the term for a star that has exhausted its nuclear fuel and collapsed into a very dense state?

context.title: Stellar evolution
context.text: The final stages of a star's life depend on its initial mass.

context.title: White dwarf
context.text: A white dwarf is what stars like the Sun become after they have exhausted their nuclear fuel.

question: Which unit is used to measure the distance between stars and galaxies?

context.title: Astronomy
context.text: Distances in space are so vast that conventional units like kilometers are impractical.

context.title: Light-year
context.text: A light-year is a unit of length used to express astronomical distances and is equivalent to about 9.46 trillion kilometers.

question: Which Nobel Prize-winning scientist discovered the effect that explains why the sky is blue and worked at the university where the 'Raman effect' was named?

context.title: Raman scattering
context.text: The Raman effect is the inelastic scattering of a photon, discovered by C. V. Raman.

context.title: C. V. Raman
context.text: Sir Chandrasekhara Venkata Raman was an Indian physicist who carried out his ground-breaking work in the field of light scattering at the University of Calcutta.

question: What is the name of the ancient city-state that defeated the Persians at the Battle of Marathon and is the capital of a country that uses the Euro?

context.title: Battle of Marathon
context.text: The Battle of Marathon took place in 490 BC, during the first Persian invasion of Greece, where the citizens of Athens defeated the Persians.

context.title: Greece
context.text: Greece is a founding member of the United Nations and joined the Eurozone in 2001; its capital is Athens.

question: Which tectonic plate's movement created the mountain range containing the highest peak in the country whose capital is Quito?

context.title: Quito
context.text: Quito is the capital of Ecuador, a country through which the Andes mountains run.

context.title: Andes
context.text: The Andes were formed by the subduction of the Nazca Plate beneath the South American Plate.

question: What is the name of the treaty that ended the war involving the 'Sun King' and redefined the borders of Europe in 1713?

context.title: Louis XIV
context.text: Louis XIV, known as the Sun King, led France during the War of the Spanish Succession.

context.title: War of the Spanish Succession
context.text: The war was concluded by the Peace of Utrecht, a series of individual peace treaties signed in the Dutch city of Utrecht in 1713.

question: Which enzyme is responsible for 'unzipping' the double helix during the process of replication in the molecule discovered by Watson and Crick?

context.title: DNA replication
context.text: In molecular biology, DNA replication is the biological process of producing two identical replicas of DNA from one original DNA molecule.

context.title: Helicase
context.text: Helicases are a class of enzymes vital to all organisms; their main function is to unpackage an organism's genes by unzipping the DNA double helix.

question: In which city is the building located that was designed by the architect who also created the glass pyramid at the Louvre?
gold_answer: Hong Kong (for Bank of China Tower) or Washington D.C. (for National Gallery)

context.title: I. M. Pei
context.text: Ieoh Ming Pei was a Chinese-American architect who designed the Louvre Pyramid and the Bank of China Tower.

context.title: Bank of China Tower
context.text: The Bank of China Tower is one of the most recognizable skyscrapers in central Hong Kong.

question: What is the primary gas found in the atmosphere of the largest planet in our solar system?

context.title: Jupiter
context.text: Jupiter is the largest planet in the Solar System and is a gas giant.

context.title: Atmosphere of Jupiter
context.text: The atmosphere of Jupiter is the largest planetary atmosphere in the Solar System, made mostly of molecular hydrogen and helium.
